question_text: Module 5 teaches that the believer grows in truth, stability, and confidence after salvation through one foundational source. According to the lesson, what is the answer to how a believer grows after salvation?
answer_options: The Word of God
Strong emotions and zeal alone
Natural gifts and talents
Sincere guesswork about God

question_text: The lesson describes the overall makeup of the Bible. According to the text, how many books does the Bible contain in total?
answer_options: It contains 66 books
It contains 39 books
It contains 27 books
It contains 12 books

question_text: John 1:1-2 is quoted to show the relationship between Jesus and the Word. According to the lesson, how is Jesus described in relation to the Word?
answer_options: He is the eternal Word revealed, not merely a messenger who brought truth
He is only a messenger who delivered God's truth to people
He is separate from the Word and stands apart from Scripture
He is one of several prophets who spoke God's words
correct_answer: He is the eternal Word revealed, not merely a messenger who brought truth

question_text: The lesson divides the Bible into two testaments and gives the book count of each. According to the text, how are the books distributed between the testaments?
answer_options: The Old Testament has 39 books and the New Testament has 27 books
The Old Testament has 27 books and the New Testament has 39 books
The Old Testament has 33 books and the New Testament has 33 books
The Old Testament has 39 books and the New Testament has 39 books
correct_answer: The Old Testament has 39 books and the New Testament has 27 books

question_text: Quoting John 8:32, the lesson explains the connection between truth and freedom. According to the text, how is freedom related to truth?
answer_options: Freedom is tied to truth, and where truth enters, deception begins to lose its grip
Freedom is produced by emotional excitement and hype
Freedom comes through ignorance of difficult matters
Freedom is achieved mainly through zeal and passion
correct_answer: Freedom is tied to truth, and where truth enters, deception begins to lose its grip

question_text: The lesson notes that the Bible is not laid out in a simple time sequence. According to the text, how is the Bible primarily arranged?
answer_options: By type, genre, and function rather than strictly by chronology
Strictly in the chronological order events occurred
Alphabetically by the names of the books
By the length of each book from longest to shortest
correct_answer: By type, genre, and function rather than strictly by chronology

question_text: The section on the Major Prophets lists Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel. According to the lesson, why are these called 'major'?
answer_options: Because of their length and scope, not because they are more inspired than the others
Because they are more inspired than the other prophetic books
Because they were written before all the other prophets
Because they contain the most prophecies about the end times
correct_answer: Because of their length and scope, not because they are more inspired than the others

question_text: The lesson describes the integrity of God's Word using passages such as Psalm 119:89, Numbers 23:19, and Isaiah 40:8. Which statement best captures the lesson's conclusion about the trustworthiness of God's Word?
answer_options: It is unchanging and stands forever, so the believer approaches it with confidence, not suspicion
It is reliable now but may weaken and expire over long periods of time
It should be approached with suspicion until each part is personally proven
It is subject to the instability of man and changes with circumstances
correct_answer: It is unchanging and stands forever, so the believer approaches it with confidence, not suspicion

question_text: Under 'Key Priorities in Gaining Knowledge,' the lesson stresses that knowledge must mature into action. According to the text, what is the danger of knowledge that does not affect the believer's life?
answer_options: It has not yet matured properly; the Word must move from hearing to believing to doing
It is automatically complete once the believer understands it correctly
It guarantees transformation in a single sitting regardless of conduct
It produces health and stability even without obedience
correct_answer: It has not yet matured properly; the Word must move from hearing to believing to doing

question_text: The lesson on building a Word culture contrasts two ways of relating to Scripture and cites Acts 20:32 about the Word building the believer. According to the text, how does a believer actually grow strong in the Word?
answer_options: By daily contact, prayerful attention, and obedience, since consistency is stronger than occasional intensity
By random passion and bursts of occasional intense reading
By touching the Bible occasionally whenever inspiration strikes
By reading large amounts quickly without praying or seeking understanding
correct_answer: By daily contact, prayerful attention, and obedience, since consistency is stronger than occasional intensity
